There is no single Linux equivalent to `WinAPI`.

For the kind of code in [`frame_win.cpp`](/home/limstudio/WorkStation/NWBLot/logger/server/frame_win.cpp), the closest low-level answer is:

- `WinAPI` windowing/UI on Windows
- `X11` or `Wayland` on Linux

But that is only the low-level window system. In practice, for app UI on Linux, people usually do not write raw X11/Wayland code the way Win32 apps use raw `CreateWindowEx`, `WNDPROC`, `WM_*`, `LISTBOX`, clipboard APIs, and so on.

My opinion for this project:

- If you want the closest conceptual replacement to raw Win32: use `X11`/`XCB` on Linux.
- If you want the sane engineering choice: use a toolkit, not raw Linux window APIs.

For `nwb_logserver`, I would recommend one of these paths:

1. `GTK` or `Qt`
   Good if you want a real desktop log window with scroll, clipboard, text wrapping, colors.

2. `ncurses`
   Good if Linux only needs a terminal log viewer. Much smaller scope.

3. Browser UI via your existing `libmicrohttpd`
   This is actually attractive here, because `nwb_logserver` already runs an HTTP server. A tiny web page for logs would be cross-platform and remove native GUI dependency entirely.

For your current codebase, I would not try to make a 1:1 Linux clone of Win32. The current Win32 frame is very manual, and Linux has no equally standard native API layer for that style. If the goal is just “show logs on Linux”, a small `ncurses` viewer or web UI is probably the best cost/benefit.

If you want, I can next map the current Win32 features in `nwb_logserver` into concrete Linux implementation options and tell you which one I’d pick for this repo.
but I also need a colouring. does ncnrses support that too?

Yes. `ncurses` supports coloring well enough for a log viewer.

What it can do:
- color text by log level: error/red, warning/yellow, info/green, debug/cyan
- combine color with attributes like `A_BOLD`, `A_REVERSE`, `A_DIM`
- color whole lines or specific spans within a line
- usually support 8, 16, or 256 colors depending on the terminal

Typical API:
```cpp
initscr();
start_color();
use_default_colors();
init_pair(1, COLOR_RED, -1);    // foreground red, default background
attron(COLOR_PAIR(1) | A_BOLD);
printw("ERROR: ...");
attroff(COLOR_PAIR(1) | A_BOLD);
```

Important limitation:
- `ncurses` is a terminal UI, not a native desktop GUI
- so you get colored text in a console window, not a Win32-style framed app with native controls
- exact colors depend on the user’s terminal theme/capabilities

For `nwb_logserver`, if your goal is “Linux log monitor with colored lines,” `ncurses` is a reasonable option.
If your goal is “same kind of desktop window as current WinAPI frame,” then `GTK`/`Qt` fits better.

My practical take: for log severity coloring, `ncurses` is enough. For a GUI-looking replacement, it is not.

If you want, I can next sketch how to map the current `nwb_logserver` features into an `ncurses` version and point out what you would lose versus the Win32 frame.
which one is lighter between GTK and Qt

I’m verifying this against current toolkit docs before answering, because “lighter” changes depending on whether you mean runtime footprint, dependency surface, or overall integration cost on Linux.

On Linux, `GTK` is usually lighter than `Qt`.

Practical meaning:
- `GTK`: lighter dependency feel, more native to Linux desktop environments, better fit if you only care about Linux.
- `Qt`: heavier overall framework, but much stronger if you want one GUI codebase for both Windows and Linux.

For your project, I’d choose like this:
- If `nwb_logserver` only needs a Linux GUI log window: `GTK`
- If you want to replace the WinAPI UI with one cross-platform GUI layer for future Windows+Linux support: `Qt`
- If you want the lightest solution overall: `ncurses` or a browser UI is lighter than both

One important nuance: “lighter” is not only RAM usage. It also means build dependencies, package size, and integration cost. On those axes, GTK usually wins on Linux. Qt can still be small enough if you only use `QtCore` + `QtGui` + `QtWidgets`, but it is generally the larger stack.

Sources:
- GTK 4 overview: https://docs.gtk.org/gtk4/overview.html
- Qt GUI overview: https://doc.qt.io/qt-6/qtgui-overview.html
- Qt Widgets overview: https://doc.qt.io/qt-6/qtwidgets-index.html

If you want, I can next give you a hard recommendation for `nwb_logserver` specifically: `GTK` vs `Qt` vs `ncurses`, based on its current WinAPI feature set.

You can implement both raw `X11` and raw `Wayland`, but I would not recommend it.

For this project, the current Win32 code in [frame_win.cpp](/home/limstudio/WorkStation/NWBLot/logger/server/frame_win.cpp) is relying on a lot of things that WinAPI gives you in one place: window creation, message loop, owner-drawn list behavior, clipboard, sizing, text drawing, and input handling. On Linux, `X11` and `Wayland` are not a single equivalent to that. They are lower-level display protocols, especially `Wayland`.

The practical problem is this:
- raw `X11` means you build much of the UI behavior yourself
- raw `Wayland` means even more of it; there are no standard widgets and no built-in text drawing API
- if you want wrapped colored text, scrolling, selection, and copy, you will likely still end up pulling in `cairo` / `pango` / `freetype` or something similar

So in the end, “avoid GTK because it is big” can turn into “build a small custom toolkit plus several graphics/text dependencies”.

My recommendation order would be:
1. `ncurses` if a terminal UI is acceptable
2. `SDL3 + SDL_ttf` if you want a real window but want to avoid GTK/Qt
3. raw `X11` only, if you absolutely want no higher-level UI dependency
4. raw `X11 + Wayland` only if you are willing to maintain two native backends

If you insist on native Linux APIs, I would start with `X11` only, not both. That will run on many Wayland desktops through `XWayland`, and it keeps scope under control. If later you decide native Wayland is necessary, you can add it behind the same `Frame` abstraction.

My actual opinion for `nwb_logserver`: `SDL3 + SDL_ttf` is probably the best compromise. It is much smaller than GTK/Qt in concept, not Linux-only, and it can replace the WinAPI frame instead of adding a separate Linux-only stack.

If you want, I can next compare `ncurses` vs `SDL3` specifically for this logger and tell you which one would be easier to integrate into the current `Frame` interface.

My recommendation is:

`SDL3` for the platform/window layer, and `Dear ImGui` only for tool UI like `nwb_logserver`.

Why this fits your repo:

- `nwb_loader` already goes through [`nwb_frame`](/home/limstudio/WorkStation/NWBLot/core/frame/CMakeLists.txt) and that path is the real Windows lock-in, not just the logger. [`loader/CMakeLists.txt`](/home/limstudio/WorkStation/NWBLot/loader/CMakeLists.txt) links `nwb_frame`, and Vulkan surface creation is hardcoded to Win32 in [`vulkan_device_manager.cpp#L1136`](/home/limstudio/WorkStation/NWBLot/core/graphics/vulkan/vulkan_device_manager.cpp#L1136).
- `nwb_logserver` needs colored, scrollable, copyable text, and the current implementation is very Win32-specific owner-draw code in [`frame_win.cpp`](/home/limstudio/WorkStation/NWBLot/logger/server/frame_win.cpp).
- SDL3 solves the shared problem once: window creation, event loop, resize/focus, clipboard, and Vulkan-capable windows on both Windows and Linux. On Linux it also hides the X11/Wayland split, so you do not maintain both.

Why not the others:

- `GLFW`: good for `nwb_loader`, weak for `nwb_logserver`. You still need to build the logger UI yourself.
- `GTK` / `Qt`: heavier than you want, and not really helping your Windows-side reuse story.
- raw `X11 + Wayland`: too much maintenance for too little gain.
- `ncurses`: only helps the logger, not `nwb_loader`.

So the practical choice is:

- Base layer: `SDL3`
- Tool UI layer: `Dear ImGui`

That gives you:
- one cross-platform window backend for `loader`, `logserver`, and likely `testbed`
- a good log viewer UI with colors, scrolling, filtering, selection, copy
- future reuse in Windows debug tools instead of a Linux-only dependency

If you want to keep it to exactly one new dependency, then choose `SDL3` alone. But the best engineering balance for this repo is `SDL3 + Dear ImGui`.

If you want, I can next sketch the concrete refactor order:
1. move `nwb_frame` from WinAPI to SDL3
2. change Vulkan surface creation to use SDL-backed window data
3. rebuild `nwb_logserver` on SDL3 + ImGui using the existing `Frame` split

Yes. On the loader side, Linux should stay simple.

[main.cpp](/home/limstudio/WorkStation/NWBLot/loader/main.cpp#L201) already has a platform-neutral `EntryPoint(...)`, and the Windows-specific part is only the final `WinMain` bridge at [main.cpp](/home/limstudio/WorkStation/NWBLot/loader/main.cpp#L244). For Linux, the loader change is just a normal `int main(int argc, char** argv)` that calls `EntryPoint(argc, argv, nullptr)`. No third-party library is needed there.

The non-trivial part is not the loader. It is the frame and Vulkan platform layer:
- [frame_win.cpp](/home/limstudio/WorkStation/NWBLot/core/frame/frame_win.cpp#L167) owns the native window loop, focus state, size query, and quit handling.
- [common.h](/home/limstudio/WorkStation/NWBLot/core/common/common.h#L142) only has `WinFrame` today, so Linux has nowhere to store native handles yet.
- [vulkan_device_manager.cpp](/home/limstudio/WorkStation/NWBLot/core/graphics/vulkan/vulkan_device_manager.cpp#L1140) only creates a Win32 surface today.
- [core/frame/CMakeLists.txt](/home/limstudio/WorkStation/NWBLot/core/frame/CMakeLists.txt#L1) only builds `frame_win.cpp`.

So the right split is:
- `loader`: trivial Linux `main`
- `core/frame`: minimal Linux window creation + event pump
- `core/graphics/vulkan`: Linux surface creation from native handles

If you want to avoid extra third-party libraries, the simplest path is a native X11/Xlib backend first, not Wayland first. Wayland without SDL/GLFW is much more work because you need compositor, surface, xdg-shell, configure/ack handling, and more protocol plumbing. Xlib can stay fairly small:
- create window
- process close/focus/configure events
- poll events each frame
- call `m_graphics.updateWindowState(...)`
- create Vulkan surface with `vkCreateXlibSurfaceKHR`

So: Linux loader side is simple, but Linux window/surface side is only “simple” if we accept a minimal native X11 backend. If that direction is acceptable, I can implement that path without adding SDL/GLFW/Qt.

in my linux setting is Fedora KDE, but will this work there too?

Yes, with one important condition: the Linux backend I added is X11-based, not native Wayland.

On Fedora KDE that means:

- If you log into a `Plasma (X11)` session, this should be the intended path.
- If you stay on the default `Plasma (Wayland)` session, the app may still run through XWayland, because [frame_linux.cpp](/home/limstudio/WorkStation/NWBLot/core/frame/frame_linux.cpp#L5) uses Xlib directly. Basic windowing/input often works there too.
- What I would not promise yet on Wayland/XWayland is stricter mouse behavior like confinement/capture semantics; Wayland is more restrictive there, and a true Wayland backend would be the proper fix.

So the short answer is: Fedora KDE is fine if you run under X11, and it may work under Wayland via XWayland for basic input, but this code is not a native Wayland solution.

If you want, I can take the next step and check the repo for any Linux build dependencies you’ll need specifically on Fedora, then give you the exact `dnf install ...` line.

Yes. For Fedora KDE, a native Wayland backend is the right long-term target, but in this repo it is a real backend project, not a small extension of the X11 work.

Right now Linux is X11-specific in several core spots:
- [core/frame/CMakeLists.txt](/home/limstudio/WorkStation/NWBLot/core/frame/CMakeLists.txt#L11) only builds one Linux frame source.
- [configuration/Dependencies.cmake](/home/limstudio/WorkStation/NWBLot/configuration/Dependencies.cmake#L4) and [configuration/X11Include.cmake](/home/limstudio/WorkStation/NWBLot/configuration/X11Include.cmake#L3) only wire X11 dependencies.
- [core/common/common.h](/home/limstudio/WorkStation/NWBLot/core/common/common.h#L160) stores `Display*` and `Window` directly in `LinuxFrame`.
- [core/graphics/vulkan/vulkan_device_manager.cpp](/home/limstudio/WorkStation/NWBLot/core/graphics/vulkan/vulkan_device_manager.cpp#L420) and [core/graphics/vulkan/vulkan_device_manager.cpp](/home/limstudio/WorkStation/NWBLot/core/graphics/vulkan/vulkan_device_manager.cpp#L1172) hardcode `VK_KHR_xlib_surface` and `vkCreateXlibSurfaceKHR`.

The clean way to do it is:
1. Refactor Linux native handles so the graphics layer stops assuming X11.
2. Split the backend into X11 and Wayland implementations, instead of growing [frame_linux.cpp](/home/limstudio/WorkStation/NWBLot/core/frame/frame_linux.cpp) into a mixed file.
3. Add Wayland deps and protocols: `wayland-client`, `xdg-shell`, `xkbcommon`, likely `wayland-cursor`, and optional pointer-constraints/relative-pointer for mouse capture.
4. Add Vulkan Wayland surface creation and handle Wayland’s configure/ack startup flow before first render.

A first pass can cover native Wayland window creation, resize, keyboard, mouse, wheel, focus, and Vulkan present. IME/dead-key composition, clipboard, and robust pointer lock should be treated as follow-up work.

If you want, I can take the first implementation slice now: backend split plus Vulkan handle refactor, which is the part that makes X11 and Wayland coexist cleanly.
yes please. proceed your work

I’m proceeding with the first Wayland slice: make Linux platform handles backend-agnostic, then split the Linux window path so X11 and Wayland can coexist instead of competing for the same ABI. After that I’ll wire Vulkan surface creation to choose Xlib or Wayland based on the live frame backend.

I have the seam isolated. The implementation is going to be four connected edits:
1. Change `LinuxFrame` from X11-native fields into a tagged opaque handle block.
2. Split Linux runtime selection so Wayland can be chosen first on a Wayland session and fall back to X11.
3. Teach Vulkan instance/surface creation to inspect that backend tag and enable `VK_KHR_xlib_surface` or `VK_KHR_wayland_surface` accordingly.
4. Add optional Wayland build wiring, including `xdg-shell` codegen and `xkbcommon`, without regressing X11-only Linux builds.

I’m starting the code edits now with the shared handle contract and dependency layer, because those are the parts every later file needs to agree on.

Implemented the first native Wayland slice alongside the existing X11 path. Linux frame data is now a tagged opaque handle block in [common.h](/home/limstudio/WorkStation/NWBLot/core/common/common.h#L151), runtime backend selection and fallback live in [frame_linux.cpp](/home/limstudio/WorkStation/NWBLot/core/frame/frame_linux.cpp#L54), the X11 loop was preserved in [frame_linux_x11.cpp](/home/limstudio/WorkStation/NWBLot/core/frame/frame_linux_x11.cpp), and a new Wayland backend now brings up an `xdg-shell` toplevel, binds seat/pointer/keyboard, translates `xkbcommon` input, handles compositor configure/close, and runs a native Wayland event loop in [frame_linux_wayland.cpp](/home/limstudio/WorkStation/NWBLot/core/frame/frame_linux_wayland.cpp#L47) and [frame_linux_wayland.cpp](/home/limstudio/WorkStation/NWBLot/core/frame/frame_linux_wayland.cpp#L839).

The build and Vulkan sides now follow that backend tag too. [WaylandInclude.cmake](/home/limstudio/WorkStation/NWBLot/configuration/WaylandInclude.cmake#L3) adds optional Wayland dependency discovery plus `xdg-shell` codegen, [core/frame/CMakeLists.txt](/home/limstudio/WorkStation/NWBLot/core/frame/CMakeLists.txt#L11) and [core/graphics/CMakeLists.txt](/home/limstudio/WorkStation/NWBLot/core/graphics/CMakeLists.txt#L41) only compile Wayland when those deps exist, and [vulkan_device_manager.cpp](/home/limstudio/WorkStation/NWBLot/core/graphics/vulkan/vulkan_device_manager.cpp#L421) plus [vulkan_device_manager.cpp](/home/limstudio/WorkStation/NWBLot/core/graphics/vulkan/vulkan_device_manager.cpp#L1193) now enable/create either Xlib or Wayland Vulkan surfaces from the same Linux frame payload.

- `/home/limstudio/WorkStation/NWBLot` is mounted `rw`
- `/home/limstudio/WorkStation/NWBLot/.git` is mounted `ro`

- `git fetch` -> `error: cannot open '.git/FETCH_HEAD': Read-only file system`
- outbound GitHub access -> `Could not resolve host: github.com`

2. shell network/DNS to GitHub is blocked.

That also explains why it worked in older sessions: those sessions were likely started with a different sandbox policy, or without the separate read-only mount on `.git`.

- outbound network to GitHub is allowed, or approvals/escalation are allowed

No, not as-is on this Linux machine.

I ran `cmake --preset linux-clang-engine-x64`, and configure stops immediately on curl: `Could NOT find CURL (missing: CURL_LIBRARY CURL_INCLUDE_DIR)`. I did not get to a full compile, because the Linux configure step itself is blocked.

The bigger issue is that current Linux builds are not actually using most vendored 3rd-party sources. On Linux, the build expects system packages instead:
- curl from the system in [3rd_parties/curl/CMakeLists.txt:80](/home/limstudio/WorkStation/NWBLot/3rd_parties/curl/CMakeLists.txt#L80)
- libmicrohttpd from the system in [3rd_parties/libmicrohttpd/CMakeLists.txt:48](/home/limstudio/WorkStation/NWBLot/3rd_parties/libmicrohttpd/CMakeLists.txt#L48)
- TBB from the system in [3rd_parties/oneTBB/CMakeLists.txt:97](/home/limstudio/WorkStation/NWBLot/3rd_parties/oneTBB/CMakeLists.txt#L97)
- zlib from the system in [3rd_parties/zlib/CMakeLists.txt:42](/home/limstudio/WorkStation/NWBLot/3rd_parties/zlib/CMakeLists.txt#L42)
- `volk` from the system or `VULKAN_SDK` in [configuration/VulkanInclude.cmake:10](/home/limstudio/WorkStation/NWBLot/configuration/VulkanInclude.cmake#L10)
- `shaderc` from the system or `VULKAN_SDK` in [configuration/ShadercInclude.cmake:10](/home/limstudio/WorkStation/NWBLot/configuration/ShadercInclude.cmake#L10)

On this box, some runtime `.so` files exist, but the development headers are missing for curl, microhttpd, TBB, xkbcommon, wayland, volk, and shaderc. Only zlib looked complete. So the current 3rd-party update is not enough by itself to make Linux workable here.

There is also one project-side Linux limitation: X11 is still mandatory. [configuration/X11Include.cmake:12](/home/limstudio/WorkStation/NWBLot/configuration/X11Include.cmake#L12) does `find_package(X11 REQUIRED)`, and both [core/frame/CMakeLists.txt:17](/home/limstudio/WorkStation/NWBLot/core/frame/CMakeLists.txt#L17) and [core/graphics/CMakeLists.txt:42](/home/limstudio/WorkStation/NWBLot/core/graphics/CMakeLists.txt#L42) always link X11 on Linux. Wayland is only additive in [configuration/WaylandInclude.cmake:48](/home/limstudio/WorkStation/NWBLot/configuration/WaylandInclude.cmake#L48).

So the answer is:

- The current Linux path is plausible, but not self-contained.
- The current 3rd-party layout is not workable on this machine without installing Linux dev packages.
- The updated vendored trees mostly do not help Linux right now, because Linux is wired to system packages instead of those vendored sources.

If you want, I can do one of two next steps:
- make Linux use vendored third parties instead of system packages
- produce the exact dependency list for this Fedora environment and then rerun the Linux configure/build check

I have the scope pinned down. I’m making three concrete changes now:

1. Stop the Linux engine preset from pulling in optional tool-only dependencies.
2. Add fallbacks for `curl` and `TBB` that use the vendored headers plus available shared libraries when the dev package metadata is missing.
3. Make the remaining Vulkan failure precise, then rerun Linux configure to see exactly what still blocks the build.

I’m editing the CMake wiring now. The changes are targeted: optional Linux tools stop poisoning the engine preset, required core dependencies get stronger fallback discovery, and the Vulkan failure becomes explicit instead of a generic missing-target error.
